summary: Cantrill argues that Contributor License Agreements (CLAs) and copyright assignments, while well-intentioned for protecting open source projects, create significant barriers to community contributions and can enable corporate misbehavior—as he learned firsthand when Oracle acquired Sun. After years of advocating against CLAs while respecting node.js leadership's autonomy on the issue, he announces that Joyent is eliminating the CLA for node.js, following the recommendation of the project's core lead after community feedback.
key_points: CLAs force contributors to navigate legal departments before contributing, creating unnecessary friction
Copyright assignments can enable acquiring companies to abuse open source projects
The illumos community learned from Oracle's acquisition of Sun to avoid CLAs entirely
Communities should make their own governance decisions, even when leaders disagree
Eliminating barriers to contribution broadens the contributor base
money_quotes: Even at their very best, CLAs and copyright assignments act as a drag on contributions as new corporate contributors are forced to seek out their legal department – which seems like asking people to go to the dentist before their pull request can be considered.
At worst, these agreements and assignments grant a corporate entity (or, as I have personally learned the hard way, its acquirer) the latitude for gross misbehavior.

While node.js is a Joyent-led project, I also believe that communities must make their own decisions – and a CLA is a sufficiently nuanced issue that reasonable people can disagree on its ultimate merits.
Doing this lowers the barrier to entry for node.js contributors thereby broadening the contributor base.
key_insight: Well-intentioned legal protections like CLAs can paradoxically harm open source communities by creating contribution friction and concentrating power that can be abused by corporate acquirers.
